coredata.dc:title: A carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for monitoring intracellular pH and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Songrui
Song, Xuerong
Hu, Zhiru
Feng, Guodong
coredata.dc:description: It is highly desired to detect intracellular pH in terms of better awareness of biological processes and early diseases diagnosis. Herein, a new fluorescent and pH-dependent carbon dots-based ratiometric probe was developed by one-pot hydrothermal method, firstly using 8-aminoquinol and citric acid (CA) as precursors. It is confirmed that the probe shows diverse emission wavelengths at 365 and 439 nm when excited at 290 nm, which can better provide self-calibration for pH detecting. Especially, the intensity ratio (I439 /I365) is linear against pH within the range of 4.2−6.4. The nanoprobe shows satisfactory pH sensitivity, selectivity, reversibility, photostability and biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and operational simplicity. The nanoprobe was successfully used as a cells-imaging label to track pH changes, indicating that it has promising application in living systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Intracellular pH monitoring
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity, stability and reversibility of CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to acknowledge Professor Zhang Kai and Associate Professor Hou Changmin, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, China, for their assistance with the TEM, DLS and XPS in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. (A) XRD pattern; (B) FT-IR spectrum (C) XPS spectrum; and high-resolution XPS spectrum of (D) C 1s, (E) N 1s, and (F) O 1s.
UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs under diﬀ ;erent excitation wavelengths.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum and (B) fluorescence intensity ratio (F365/F439) of CDs in BR buffer from pH = 3.2 to pH = 8.0 (λex = 290 nm).

(A) Fluorescence intensity changes of CDs in BR buffer at pH 4.5 and 7.0 with different interfering substances (1 mM), respectively. (λex = 290 nm); (B) fluorescence reversibility with pH between 3.0 and 8.0, repeatedly.
Fluorescence imaging of MCF-7 cells cultured with 60 μg /mL carbon dots in buffer solution with various pH.
Fluorescence imaging of 60 μg /mL carbon dots incubated with MCF-7 cells cultured with CQ and DEX.
Schematic of the synthesis of the probe and its utilization for cells imaging.

head.ce:title: A carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescence probe for monitoring intracellular pH and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is highly desired to detect intracellular pH in terms of better awareness of biological processes and early diseases diagnosis. Herein, a new fluorescent and pH-dependent carbon dots-based ratiometric probe was developed by one-pot hydrothermal method, firstly using 8-aminoquinol and citric acid (CA) as precursors. It is confirmed that the probe shows diverse emission wavelengths at 365 and 439 nm when excited at 290 nm, which can better provide self-calibration for pH detecting. Especially, the intensity ratio (I439 /I365) is linear against pH within the range of 4.2−6.4. The nanoprobe shows satisfactory pH sensitivity, selectivity, reversibility, photostability and biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and operational simplicity. The nanoprobe was successfully used as a cells-imaging label to track pH changes, indicating that it has promising application in living systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular pH, as an indication of cell function changes, is of the essence in various biological processes, including cellular proliferation, apoptosis, enzyme activity, endocytosis and so on [1–5]. And there are many regulating systems of organisms that are related to acid-base dynamic equilibrium [6]. As a result, anomalous intracellular pH values may cause ineffectiveness of organelles and even diseases, cancer, Alzheimer's disease, for example [7,8]. Therefore, it’s high time to develop strategies competent for intracellular pH measurement as well as applied in the early disease diagnosis.

There are numerous methods having been proposed for monitoring the intracellular pH, such as the voltammetry [9], the inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [10], high-performance liquid chromatography [11], the atomic emission spectroscopy [12] etc. In particular, ﬂuorescent based sensors including organic dyes, and ﬂuorescent proteins [13–15], are highly exploited and have attracted ongoing interests in recent years due to their good sensitivity, spatial and temporal resolution, noninvasiveness and fast response. However, there are some limiting factors such as low water solubility, rapid photobleaching and tedious synthetic process that need to be improved [16–18]. To overcome these problems, carbon dots (CDs) has emerged as a new nanomaterial, which can satisfy the above needs with the advantages of good photostability, biocompatibility [19,20], reversibility, ease of operation as well as low toxicity [21,22]. Consequently, they are broadly utilized in sensor [23,24], bioimaging [25–29] and other areas. Up to now, various CDs have been constructed via a bottom up approach adopting heterogeneous materials, nevertheless, a few of them are used for detecting pH values inside cells [30–32]. Besides, ratiometric fluorescence probes having two different emission intensity can ward off many objective factors that may lead to the reduction of the reliability of the experimental results, such as the stability of the instrument, concentration of the CDs and so on when compared with other CDs having single emission [33,34].

Herein, inspired by above reports, a new fluorescent and pH-induced carbon dots-based ratiometric probe was synthesized by using 8-aminoquinol as the N source, and CA as the C source. This pH probe was developed using one-pot carbonization hydrothermal approach. It is worth noting that 8-aminoquinol here was first adopted as the N source for the synthesis of carbon dots. It is because the CDs are coated by quinolyl nitrogen which can be protonated or deprotonated in the process of combining with H+ or OH− in solution that renders this probe as a good candidate for detecting pH change in cell microenvironment [35,36]. After systemic investigation, all the results show that this pH probe has satisfactory pH sensitivity, selectivity, reversibility, photostability, low cytotoxicity, and operational simplicity. Besides, the ratios of F439/F365 also displayed a good linear response to pH 4.2–6.4. All these eligible properties demonstrating that they can ratiometrically response to pH and be applied in cells imaging.

The carbon dots were synthesized by one−step hydrothermal approach. First, 0.07 g 8-aminoquinol and 0.9 g CA were joined into 10 mL distilled water and then mixed under vigorous stirring. Secondly, the solution was poured into a lined stainless-steel autoclave (15 mL) and reacted at 200 ℃ for 6 h. The product was cooled to room temperature naturally before preliminarily purified through a 0.45 μm microporous membrane to discard big particles. Then, a 2000 Da dialysis bag was used about 48 h (change water every 6 h) for further purification. Finally, 0.5025 g black solid product was gained via freeze-drying under vacuum obtained a yield of 51.80 %. Repeatability of CDs is also determined. As Fig. S1 shown, CDs synthesized three times respectively have the similar emission and excitation wavelength showing the unspecific preparation of CDs.

CDs were dissolved in deionized (DI) water to prepare stock solutions (5 mg mL−1). Subsequently, the pH-dependent ﬂuorescence property of the probe was explored in Britton-Robinson (BR) solution at different pH values (pH = 3.0–8.0), the reversibility of the CDs was determined between pH = 3.0 and 8.0 by adding HCl and NaOH solutions. And 290 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength. All the pH sensing processes were performed at room temperature.

For the cytotoxicity determination, MCF-7 cells were first incubated in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) including fetal bovine serum (FBS, 9 %) and streptomycin (1 %) at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 for 24 h in a 96-well plate prior to the experiment. Then, various concentrations of the probe were joined to wells and cultured with cells for additional 24 h. Afterwards, 10 μL Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was added to each well before the final cells were cultivated for another 2 h. At last, absorbance at 450 nm was measured by a microplate reader.

MCF-7 cells were first incubated on sterile coverslips placed in a six-well plate and made to attach for 24 h before the experiment. Subsequently, MCF-7 cells were exposed to 60 μg mL−1 carbon dots and cultured for another 4 h. Then the media was replaced by high-K+ HEPES-buffered solution which contains 5 μg mL-1 nigericin with diverse pH (4.0, 5.2, 6.0 and 7.4) for 30 min. After the discard of solution, the cells were washed by PBS buffer for three times and then dealt with 1 mL 3.7 ％ paraformaldehyde to fix the cells. Finally, cell imaging was carried on a confocal microscope.

Here, chloroquine (CQ) as an antimalarial drug that can result in an increase of intracellular pH [37] and dexamethasone (DEX) which can lead to apoptosis along with a decline of pH [38] were selected as drug-stimulant. MCF-7 cells were cultured with 200 μM CQ for 1 h and 1 μM DEX for 6 h, respectively. Then, the medium was replaced by fresh culture solution containing 60 μg mL−1 CDs and MCF-7 cells continued to be incubated for 4 h at 37 ℃. At last, the cells were washed with PBS for three times and then dealt with 1 mL 3.7 ％ paraformaldehyde to fix the cells. Finally, cell imaging was carried on a confocal microscope.

Among them, x is the CDs dissolved in BR buffer at pH = 3.0 and 7.0 respectively and st means quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4. D is the integrated area of fluorescence emission spectrum excited at 350 nm. A is the UV–vis absorbance value, and n is the refractive index of the solvent we used. Quinine sulfate was selected as the standard [39].

FT-IR was used to characterize the functional groups of CDs. From the spectrum (Fig. 2B), we found that CDs were composed of plenty functional groups including -NH2 (3419 and 3103 cm−1), CONH (1710 cm−1) which comes from the amide reaction between citric acid and 8-aminoquinoline, CN (1404 cm−1) and CC (1214 cm−1). XPS was used to further detect chemical compositions of CDs. Fig. 2C indicates the presence of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s locating at 284.66, 399.5 and 532.19 eV, respectively. Two obvious peaks can be observed in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) at 284.5 and 285.1 responding to CC and CC. And the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) is resolved into three peaks at 398.9, 399.6, and 401.3 eV, which are the characteristic peaks of pyridinium N, amino N, and graphitic N, indicating that CDs have been carbonized to some extent in the composition course. Besides, the deconvolution of the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2F) displayed two peaks at 531.9 and 532.5 eV, suggesting the formation of CO and CO. Otherwise, the result of element analysis shows that the content of C、N、O atom in carbon dots is 45.60 %, 1.81 % and 47.78 % respectively. Taken together, the analysis above demonstrates that the CDs are mostly consist of C, N, O elements, coated by a variety of functional groups on the surface and 8-aminoquinoline have already reacted with CA to produce CDs.

The optical features of the probe were measured by UV–vis and ﬂuorescence spectra. According to Fig. 3 , it is evident that there are two absorption peaks at 270 nm and 313 nm which may origin from the π→ π* transitions of CC and the n→ π* transitions of the CO and CN bonds, respectively [41,42]. Besides, the location of the emission peak moves negligibly as excitation wavelengths changes with interval of 20 nm in Fig. 3 and the excitation peak is also nearly unchanged as the emission wavelength increases (Figure. S3) corresponding to their excitation-independent PL properties. Fluorescence intensity is maximal when excited at 290 nm which is set as the excitation wavelength in follow-up experiments. QY of CDs was measured to reach a value of 6.50 % at pH = 3.0 and 9.77 % at pH = 7.0 which is consistent with the change of fluorescent emission intensity at different pH.

In the past years, probes bearing quinolyl have been successfully applied because of the protonation and deprotonation of quinolyl nitrogen in the process of combining with H+ or OH− in solution [35,36], but it has never been employed in CDs. Therefore, 8-aminoquinol and CA herein were firstly combined through one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize CDs coated by pyridinium on the surface. As Fig. 4 A represents, it is remarkable that ﬂuorescence intensity at 365 nm distinctly fades away with pH values decreasing from 8.0 to 3.2, while that at 439 nm displays a gentle increase. Noticeably, there is a red shift in emission with acidity increasing which may result from the protonation of quinolyl nitrogen [36]. Furthermore, Fig. 4B shows a sigmoidal ﬁtting between emission intensity ratios of F365/F439 and pH which has the highest response between approx. 4.5 and 6.3. They also exhibit a great linear response to pH 4.2–6.4 with an equation of F = - 0.55× pH + 3.90 (R2 = 0.9913) (Fig. S4). According to the above results, all these attractive characters endow CDs with the potential of pH detection, which is consistent with the anticipation.

In order to verify our assumption that this observed pH responsive feature attributes to the protonation and deprotonation of quinolyl nitrogen on the surface of CDs as described above, the zeta potentials under different pH BR buffer were explored. As Figure. S5 shows, it is -4.02 mV under acidic solution (pH = 3) and a more negative value of -17.00 mV is measured at pH 7, which agrees with our expectation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reversibility of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Eligible selectivity of CDs is of great significance for their potential as biological sensors. From Fig. 5 A, it is evident that there is no significant difference in fluorescence intensity under the interference of some ions (Zn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+,Na+, K+, Al3+, Fe3+ NH4 +, Cl− and ClO−), amino acids (Glu, Arg, Asp, His), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), homocysteine (Hcy), H2O2, ascorbic acid(AA) at pH 4.5 and 7, proving the good anti-interference capability of this probe. Meanwhile, in order to assess the photostability of the nanoprobe, CDs were exposed to ultraviolet radiation over a period of 30 min at pH = 5.5 and 7.0. As Fig. S6 displayed, only some inappreciable changes in fluorescence intensity can be observed, suggesting their satisfactory photostability under sensing conditions. In addition, the impact of ionic concentration on the stability of the probe was researched at NaCl solution when the concentration is up to 1.0 mol/L, and fluorescence intensity was almost constant in Fig. S7, which reveals the stability of CDs in high ionic strength. The reversibility of CDs was also measured, and good reversibility is found in Fig. 5B as the pH is cycled between pH = 3 and 8. Taken together, CDs developed here is proved to be a hopeful fluorescence nanoprobe for sensing applications (Scheme 1 ).

To investigate the possible application of the probe for intracellular imaging, Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) reagent was applied to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs and the absorbance of samples at 450 nm was detected by a microplate reader during the detection process. The test of MCF-7 cells cultivated with CCK-8 in the absence of CDs was used as control. Figure S8 depicts that the cell viability of MCF-7 cells only with CCK-8 is as 100 %, and the cell viability of MCF-7 cells with different concentrations of CDs solution is also shown in it. All the cell samples were cultivated for 24 h. It is easily found that more than 80 % of cells survived even the dosage of 80 μg mL−1 CDs, verifying the eligible biocompatibility of this CDs probe.

Encouraged by the above successful consequences, CDs were next applied for MCF-7 cells imaging. As Fig. 6 displayed, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in MCF-7 cells progressively enhanced with the pH increasing from 4.0 to 8.0. In Fig. S10, the relative mean fluorescent intensity of CDs in MCF-7 cells at different pH levels can be roughly quantified using the software called Image J [43] from fluorescent images of MCF-7 cell. The CDs display a pH-dependent property in living cells with a good linear relationship in the range of pH 4.0–8.0, which is corresponding to the result of fluorescence spectrum.

Furthermore, in order to confirm whether this nanoprobe is competent for real-time sensing of pH in living cells, the dynamic fluorescence imaging of the probe was performed employing different drugs, such as CQ and DEX. As shown in Fig. 7 , compared with the cells cultured in pH = 7.4 solution, the fluorescence intensity of MCF-7 cells treated with CQ is higher and that of cells stimulated by DEX is relatively dim. Taken together, CDs developed here are qualified as a nanoprobe for real-time detecting pH changes in dysfunctional cells.

In conclusion, a new fluorescent and pH-induced carbon dots-based ratiometric probe firstly using 8-aminoquinol as the N source was developed successfully for intracellular pH values detecting. With the advantages of satisfactory pH sensitivity, selectivity, reversibility, photostability and biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and operational simplicity, CDs are competent for ratiometricly detecting intracellular pH variation, which can provide self-calibration for environmental disturbances. All above results indicate the promising applications of CDs for the diagnosis of pH-associated diseases and disorders.

Songrui Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Xuerong Song: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhiru Hu: Visualization, Investigation. Guodong Feng: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge Professor Zhang Kai and Associate Professor Hou Changmin, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, China, for their assistance with the TEM, DLS and XPS in this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113129.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a Series of Practical Fluorescent Chemical Tools To Measure pH Values in Living Samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Takahashi, Y. Kagami, K. Hanaoka, T. Terai, T. Komatsu, T. Ueno, M. Uchiyama, I. Koyama-Honda, N. Mizushima, T. Taguchi, H. Arai, T. Nagano, Y. Urano, Development of a Series of Practical Fluorescent Chemical Tools To Measure pH Values in Living Samples, J Am Chem Soc, 140 (2018) 5925-5933.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A zero cross-talk ratiometric two-photon probe for imaging of acid pH in living cells and tissues and early detection of tumor in mouse model

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Xu, Y. Li, C.Y. Poon, H.N. Chan, H.W. Li, M.S. Wong, A Zero Cross-Talk Ratiometric Two-Photon Probe for Imaging of Acid pH in Living Cells and Tissues and Early Detection of Tumor in Mouse Model, Anal Chem, 90 (2018) 8800-8806.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-depth fluorescence imaging using a two-photon FRET system for mitochondrial pH in live cells and tissues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.J. Chang, K. Kim, K.S. Park, J.S. Kang, C.S. Lim, H.M. Kim, C. Kang, M.H. Lee, High-depth fluorescence imaging using a two-photon FRET system for mitochondrial pH in live cells and tissues, Chem Commun (Camb), 54 (2018) 13531-13534.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-modal Colorimetric/Fluorescence molecular probe for ratiometric sensing of pH and its application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wu, X. Li, C. Huang, N. Jia, Dual-Modal Colorimetric/Fluorescence Molecular Probe for Ratiometric Sensing of pH and Its Application, Anal Chem, 88 (2016) 8332-8338.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal conversion of Magnolia liliiflora into nitrogen-doped carbon dots as an effective turn-off fluorescence sensing, multi-colour cell imaging and fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T. Edison, K.R. Aseer, S. Perumal, Y.R. Lee, Hydrothermal conversion of Magnolia liliiflora into nitrogen-doped carbon dots as an effective turn-off fluorescence sensing, multi-colour cell imaging and fluorescent ink, Colloid Surf. B-Biointerfaces, 169 (2018) 321-328.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Indian gooseberry-derived tunable fluorescent carbon dots as a promise for in Vitro/In vivo multicolor bioimaging and fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Atchudan, T. Edison, S. Perumal, Y.R. Lee, Indian Gooseberry-Derived Tunable Fluorescent Carbon Dots as a Promise for In Vitro/In Vivo Multicolor Bioimaging and Fluorescent Ink, ACS Omega, 3 (2018) 17590-17601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red/orange dual-emissive carbon dots for pH sensing and cell imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R. Zhang, R.G. Su, J. Zhong, L. Fei, W. Cai, Q.W. Guan, W.J. Li, N. Li, Y.S. Chen, L.L. Cai, Q. Xu, Red/orange dual-emissive carbon dots for pH sensing and cell imaging, Nano Res., 12 (2019) 815-821.
